claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start with the most important one:

**Do you have a child or children you're responsible for? If yes, how old are they?**

(Please note that eligibility is assessed separately for each child, so if you have multiple children, please let me know all of their ages.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who is 17 years old. I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for them and would like to know if I'm eligible.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 17 years old, they fall into the 16-19 age category, which means there are additional criteria to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their education or training status. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or BTECs)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently studying for A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful, thank you! A-levels are an approved qualification for Child Benefit purposes.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! A-levels are an approved qualification. Now I need to check a couple more things about Alex's education:

**Is Alex studying full-time (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is studying full-time at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now I need to check one more thing about Alex's work situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex does work quite a bit – they have a part-time job at a local shop alongside their studies. They work full-time hours, so that's 24 hours or more per week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. Let me check the next criteria:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify something important here. Let me check the rules more carefully.

Looking at the official guidance, the key issue is whether a child aged 16-19 is working 24 hours or more per week. However, I need to understand Alex's situation better:

**You mentioned Alex is studying A-levels full-time AND working 24+ hours per week. Can you confirm:**
1. **Is Alex still enrolled in and actively attending their A-level courses?**
2. **Are they definitely working 24 hours or more per week?**

This is important because the rules state that children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week are generally not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're also in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex is definitely still enrolled and actively attending their A-level courses full-time at college. And yes, they are working 24 hours or more per week at the shop – they usually work evenings and weekends to fit it around their studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming those details. I now have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've provided:

- Alex is 17 years old
- Alex is studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college (which is approved education)
- Alex is working 24 hours or more per week

According to the UK government rules, **children aged 16 or over who are working 24 or more hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit**, even if they are also in approved education or training.

This means that unfortunately, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time because they are working full-time hours (24+ hours per week).

**However**, if Alex's working hours were to reduce to less than 24 hours per week while they continue their A-level studies, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for them again, as they would then meet all the eligibility criteria.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit eligibility, or do you have any other children you'd like to check eligibility for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I see, that's disappointing to hear, but thank you for explaining it clearly. So even though Alex is still in full-time education doing A-levels, because they're working 24 hours or more per week, I can't claim Child Benefit for them. That's good to know – at least if their hours drop below 24 hours a week, I could claim again. No, that's all I needed to know. Thank you for your help.
